agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement. The compliance mandate for immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, and field-level change tracking aligns precisely with event sourcing's core strengths. However, full event sourcing introduces significant operational complexity that may be overkill when the primary driver is audit compliance rather than complex domain behavior. I recommend a hybrid approach: event sourcing for the loan application lifecycle (where the audit trail is the primary read model) combined with traditional CRUD for supporting contexts like user management. This delivers the compliance guarantees without forcing event sourcing onto the entire platform.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts loan application mutations (status changes, data updates, officer assignments). Validates commands against current state and business rules, then emits domain events. Rejects invalid transitions (e.g., approved → submitted).","boundaries":"Owns the write side of loan applications. No direct database reads for queries. Loads aggregate state by replaying events from the event store."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all loan application domain events. Each event includes aggregate ID, sequence number, event type, payload (changed fields with before/after values), actor ID, timestamp, and correlation ID. Supports optimistic concurrency via sequence numbers.","boundaries":"The single source of truth for loan application state. No UPDATE or DELETE operations permitted. Schema enforcement at the database level (immutable table with no update/delete grants)."},{"name":"Audit Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the event stream into compliance-friendly views: full change history per application, point-in-time state reconstruction, field-level diff reports, actor audit trails. Optimized for the queries the compliance team actually runs.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent with the event store. Rebuilt from scratch if corrupted or if new audit dimensions are needed. Read-only."},{"name":"Current State Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the latest state of each loan application for operational use (dashboards, search, officer workflows). Replaces the current loan_applications table for read purposes.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent. Can be denormalized for query performance. This is what the UI reads from."},{"name":"Snapshot Store","responsibility":"Periodically stores serialized aggregate state at a known event sequence number to avoid replaying the full event history on every command. Snapshots are an optimization, not a source of truth.","boundaries":"Optional cache layer. Can be rebuilt from events. Stale snapshots are safe — replay continues from snapshot sequence number."},{"name":"Projection Rebuilder","responsibility":"Tooling to replay the full event stream and rebuild any read model from scratch. Critical for adding new audit dimensions retroactively and for disaster recovery of read models.","boundaries":"Batch infrastructure. Must handle large event volumes efficiently. Should be idempotent."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Loan Command Service","contract":"POST /loans/{id}/commands/{commandType} — payload varies by command type (SubmitApplication, UpdateField, ChangeStatus, AssignOfficer). Returns 202 Accepted with event IDs.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Loan Command Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"append(aggregateId, expectedVersion, events[]) — optimistic concurrency via expectedVersion. Returns new version number or ConflictException.","coupling_type":"tight (intentional — command service owns the store)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Audit Read Model","contract":"Event subscription (poll or push). Events delivered in order per aggregate. Consumer tracks its own checkpoint position.","coupling_type":"loose (async, consumer-driven)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Current State Read Model","contract":"Same subscription mechanism as audit read model. Independent checkpoint.","coupling_type":"loose (async, consumer-driven)"},{"from":"Compliance UI","to":"Audit Read Model","contract":"GET /audit/loans/{id}/history — full event history. GET /audit/loans/{id}/state?asOf={timestamp} — point-in-time reconstruction. GET /audit/loans/{id}/field-changes?field={name} — field-level history.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Loan Command Service","to":"Snapshot Store","contract":"loadSnapshot(aggregateId) returns (state, version) or null. saveSnapshot(aggregateId, state, version). Best-effort — failures don't block commands.","coupling_type":"loose (optional optimization)"}],"data_flow":"1) User action hits Command Service → 2) Service loads aggregate (from snapshot + subsequent events) → 3) Command validated against current state and business rules → 4) Domain events emitted and appended to Event Store (single atomic write, optimistic concurrency) → 5) Event subscriptions push events to projection handlers → 6) Audit Read Model and Current State Read Model updated asynchronously → 7) Compliance team queries Audit Read Model for history/reconstruction; Operations team queries Current State Read Model for workflows.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing","rationale":"Core pattern. The compliance requirement IS the event log — events are the primary source of truth, not a derived artifact. This makes the audit trail a first-class architectural concern rather than an afterthought bolted onto CRUD."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Natural companion to event sourcing. Separates the write model (event stream) from read models (materialized projections). Allows audit-optimized and operations-optimized read models to coexist without compromising either."},{"pattern":"Aggregate (DDD)","rationale":"LoanApplication aggregate protects status transition invariants and ensures events are only emitted from valid state transitions. One aggregate per loan application, identified by application ID."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency","rationale":"Prevents lost updates when multiple officers act on the same application. expectedVersion on append ensures no events are missed between load and write."},{"pattern":"Materialized View / Projection","rationale":"Multiple read models built from the same event stream, each optimized for its consumer (compliance vs. operations vs. analytics)."},{"pattern":"Snapshotting","rationale":"Performance optimization for aggregates with long event histories. Loan applications that go through many revisions would otherwise require replaying hundreds of events per command."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":[{"name":"LoanApplication","root_entity":"LoanApplication (identified by applicationId)","invariants":["Status transitions must follow valid state machine (e.g., Draft→Submitted→UnderReview→Approved/Denied/ReturnedForInfo)","Required fields must be present before submission","Only assigned officer can approve/deny","Denied applications cannot be re-approved without a new submission","All mutations must capture actor identity"],"events":["ApplicationCreated","ApplicationSubmitted","FieldUpdated(fieldName, oldValue, newValue)","StatusChanged(fromStatus, toStatus, reason)","OfficerAssigned(officerId)","DocumentAttached(documentId, documentType)","ApplicationApproved(conditions[])","ApplicationDenied(reason)","ApplicationWithdrawn(reason)"],"value_objects":["ApplicationStatus","ApplicantInfo","LoanTerms","Address","EmploymentInfo","CreditScore"]}]},{"name":"Audit & Compliance","classification":"supporting","aggregates":[{"name":"AuditTrail (read model, not a true aggregate)","root_entity":"Projection built from LoanApplication events","invariants":["Immutability — no modification of recorded history","Completeness — every event must be captured","Traceability — every entry must have actor and timestamp"],"events":[],"value_objects":["AuditEntry","PointInTimeSnapshot","FieldChangeRecord"]}]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":[{"name":"User/Officer","root_entity":"User","invariants":["Authentication and authorization"],"events":[],"value_objects":["Role","Permission"]}]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","downstream":"Audit & Compliance","relationship":"Published Language — Loan Application Lifecycle publishes domain events in a well-defined schema. Audit & Compliance subscribes and projects into compliance-specific views. No back-pressure or influence on the upstream model."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","relationship":"Conformist — Loan Application Lifecycle uses user/officer IDs from IAM as-is to record actor identity in events. No translation needed."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Adopt event sourcing for loan application lifecycle","gains":"Immutable audit trail as a first-class citizen (not bolted on). Full point-in-time reconstruction by replaying events to any timestamp. Field-level change tracking built into the event model. Regulatory compliance becomes an architectural property rather than an application-level concern. Retroactive addition of new audit dimensions by replaying history.","sacrifices":"Significant increase in operational complexity vs. current CRUD model. Team must learn event sourcing patterns and pitfalls. Eventual consistency between write and read models requires careful UX handling. Event schema evolution becomes a long-term maintenance concern. Higher storage requirements (all events retained forever).","alternatives_considered":["CDC (Change Data Capture) on existing table — captures row-level changes via database log (e.g., Debezium on PostgreSQL WAL). Simpler operationally but loses semantic meaning (you see column diffs, not domain events). Point-in-time reconstruction requires replaying CDC log which is lower-level than domain events. Viable if the team lacks event sourcing experience.","Audit trigger table — database triggers write to an audit_log table on every INSERT/UPDATE. Simple to implement, but tightly coupled to schema, loses semantic context (why the change happened), and triggers can be disabled or bypassed. Does not naturally support point-in-time reconstruction.","Temporal tables (SQL:2011) — system-versioned tables that automatically maintain history. PostgreSQL doesn't natively support this (requires extensions like temporal_tables). Good for point-in-time queries but doesn't capture actor or intent. Limited to table-level, not domain-level semantics."]},{"decision":"CQRS with separate read models for audit and operations","gains":"Each consumer gets an optimized view. Audit queries don't impact operational performance. New reporting dimensions can be added by creating new projections without touching the write side.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — a status change might take milliseconds to appear in read models. More infrastructure to operate (projection handlers, checkpoint tracking). Must handle projection failures and replay.","alternatives_considered":["Single read model serving both audit and operations — simpler but forces compromise on query optimization. Audit queries (full history, point-in-time) have very different access patterns than operational queries (current state, search)."]},{"decision":"Hybrid approach — event sourcing only for loan applications, CRUD for supporting contexts","gains":"Limits complexity to where it delivers value. Supporting contexts (user management, notifications) stay simple. Team can adopt incrementally.","sacrifices":"Two persistence paradigms to maintain. Must be disciplined about which contexts use which approach.","alternatives_considered":["Full event sourcing across all contexts — unnecessary complexity for generic/supporting subdomains. User profile changes don't need immutable audit trails."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Read model projections","evolution_path":"New compliance requirements (e.g., new reporting dimensions, new regulatory queries) can be met by adding new projections that replay the existing event stream. No changes to the write side needed. This is the single biggest advantage for a regulated domain."},{"area":"Event schema","evolution_path":"Events should be versioned from day one (e.g., ApplicationCreated_v1). Upcasters transform old event versions to current schema during replay. This allows the domain model to evolve without rewriting history."},{"area":"Integration with external systems","evolution_path":"The event stream can feed downstream systems (credit bureaus, document management, regulatory reporting) via event subscriptions without modifying the core domain."},{"area":"Analytics and ML","evolution_path":"The complete event history is a rich dataset for loan processing analytics, officer performance metrics, and predictive models. New analytical projections can be added at any time."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event store immutability","reason":"Events must never be modified or deleted. This is both a compliance requirement and an architectural invariant. The database schema should enforce this with restricted grants (INSERT only, no UPDATE/DELETE). This rigidity is intentional and valuable."},{"area":"Event schema backward compatibility","reason":"Once an event type is published, its schema becomes a contract. Breaking changes require new event versions and upcasters. Removing fields from existing event types is forbidden. This discipline is essential but adds friction to schema changes."},{"area":"Aggregate identity","reason":"The applicationId is the partition key for the event stream. Changing how applications are identified (e.g., merging two applications) is extremely difficult in an event-sourced system. Get the aggregate boundaries right upfront."},{"area":"Actor identity in events","reason":"Every event must capture who performed the action. This must be enforced at the command service level before events are created. Retrofitting actor identity onto existing events is impossible (immutability)."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team inexperience with event sourcing leads to implementation mistakes (e.g., mutable events, missing concurrency control, projections that lose events)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in training. Start with a spike/proof-of-concept on a single aggregate. Use a proven event store library or database pattern rather than building from scratch. Consider Marten (if .NET), Axon (if JVM), or EventStoreDB as purpose-built solutions."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution becomes painful over time as the domain model changes","severity":"high","mitigation":"Version all events from day one. Build upcaster infrastructure before you need it. Establish event schema review as part of the PR process. Never modify existing event schemas — always create new versions."},{"risk":"Eventual consistency confuses users (e.g., officer changes status but dashboard still shows old status)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use read-your-own-writes pattern: after a command succeeds, return the expected new state directly from the command response (or poll the read model with a minimum version expectation). Most projections will update within milliseconds."},{"risk":"Storage growth from retaining all events forever","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Events are typically small (< 1KB each). A loan application with 200 events over its lifetime is ~200KB. Even millions of applications produce manageable storage. Use compression on the event payload column. Archive old events to cold storage if needed, but never delete."},{"risk":"Projection rebuild time grows as event volume increases","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition events by aggregate type. Build projections with parallel consumers. Use snapshots for frequently-accessed aggregates. Projection rebuilds are infrequent (only needed for schema changes or new projections) and can run as batch jobs."},{"risk":"Regulatory requirement to delete personal data (e.g., GDPR right to erasure) conflicts with event immutability","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Use crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in events with per-customer keys. To 'delete' data, destroy the encryption key. The events remain but PII becomes unrecoverable. This must be designed in from the start — retrofitting is extremely difficult. Confirm with legal whether financial services regulations exempt loan data from deletion requirements (many do for the loan lifecycle period)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement event sourcing for the LoanApplication aggregate with a PostgreSQL-backed event store (single events table with aggregate_id, sequence_number, event_type, event_data JSONB, actor_id, timestamp, correlation_id). Add a unique constraint on (aggregate_id, sequence_number) for optimistic concurrency. Restrict table grants to INSERT only. Build two projections: current-state (replaces existing loan_applications table for reads) and audit-trail (optimized for compliance queries). Phase 2: Add point-in-time reconstruction query support to the audit projection. Phase 3: Add event schema versioning and upcaster infrastructure. Run the old CRUD system in parallel during migration, with a reconciliation job comparing states.","files_to_create_or_modify":["events_table.sql — DDL for the event store table with INSERT-only grants and proper indexing","loan_application_aggregate.{ext} — Aggregate root with state machine, event application methods, and command handlers","event_store_repository.{ext} — Repository that loads aggregates by replaying events (with snapshot optimization) and appends new events with optimistic concurrency","domain_events.{ext} — Event type definitions with versioning (ApplicationCreated_v1, FieldUpdated_v1, StatusChanged_v1, etc.)","current_state_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes latest loan application state into a queryable table","audit_trail_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes full change history with field-level diffs and point-in-time reconstruction support","projection_checkpoint.{ext} — Tracks last-processed event position per projection for reliable catch-up","command_handlers.{ext} — HTTP/API layer that deserializes commands, loads aggregates, and invokes command methods","migration_reconciliation_job.{ext} — Temporary: compares event-sourced state with legacy CRUD table to validate correctness during migration"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each command handler follows: load aggregate (from snapshot + events) → validate command → apply domain logic → emit events → append to store → return success. Projections follow: subscribe to event stream → for each event, update read model → checkpoint position. All events are immutable value objects with version annotations. Aggregate protects all invariants — no business logic in command handlers or projections.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in projections — projections are dumb transformers that map events to read model state","Do NOT allow direct writes to read model tables — they must only be updated by projections","Do NOT skip optimistic concurrency — without it, concurrent commands can produce inconsistent event streams","Do NOT store derived data in events — events should capture facts (what happened), not computations","Do NOT use the event store as a message bus — if you need to notify other systems, publish events to a separate message broker after they are persisted","Do NOT forget to include actor identity (who) and correlation ID (why/which request) in every event","Do NOT make event schemas nullable 'just in case' — be explicit about what each event version contains","Do NOT underestimate the migration — run old and new systems in parallel with reconciliation before cutting over"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong fit for immutable audit trail requirements in financial services","CQRS with separate audit and operational read models is the right separation","LoanApplication is the correct aggregate boundary","Crypto-shredding is the standard approach for PII in event-sourced systems","Hybrid approach (event sourcing only where needed) reduces unnecessary complexity"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choices (EventStoreDB vs PostgreSQL-backed vs framework) depend on the team's stack and expertise which is unknown","Whether GDPR right-to-erasure applies to this loan data (jurisdiction and regulation dependent)","Expected event volume and whether snapshotting is needed from day one vs. added later","Team's current skill level with event sourcing patterns"],"assumptions":["The team uses a relational database (PostgreSQL assumed) and can implement the event store as a table rather than requiring a purpose-built event store product","Loan applications have a bounded lifecycle (months, not decades) keeping per-aggregate event counts manageable","The compliance team's query patterns are primarily per-application (not cross-application analytics), which aligns well with aggregate-partitioned event streams","The platform is not already using an event-driven architecture — this would be a new paradigm for the team"]}}

content.thinking: Let me analyze this event sourcing evaluation for a financial services loan application audit trail.

content.text: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement. The compliance mandate for immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, and field-level change tracking aligns precisely with event sourcing's core strengths. However, full event sourcing introduces significant operational complexity that may be overkill when the primary driver is audit compliance rather than complex domain behavior. I recommend a hybrid approach: event sourcing for the loan application lifecycle (where the audit trail is the primary read model) combined with traditional CRUD for supporting contexts like user management. This delivers the compliance guarantees without forcing event sourcing onto the entire platform.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts loan application mutations (status changes, data updates, officer assignments). Rejects invalid transitions (e.g., approved → submitted).","boundaries":"Owns the write side of loan applications. No direct database reads for queries. Loads aggregate state by replaying events from the event store."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all loan application domain events. Each event includes aggregate ID, sequence number, event type, payload (changed fields with before/after values), actor ID, timestamp, and correlation ID. Supports optimistic concurrency via sequence numbers.","boundaries":"The single source of truth for loan application state. No UPDATE or DELETE operations permitted. Schema enforcement at the database level (immutable table with no update/delete grants)."},{"name":"Audit Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the event stream into compliance-friendly views: full change history per application, point-in-time state reconstruction, field-level diff reports, actor audit trails. Optimized for the queries the compliance team actually runs.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent with the event store. Rebuilt from scratch if corrupted or if new audit dimensions are needed. Read-only."},{"name":"Current State Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the latest state of each loan application for operational use (dashboards, search, officer workflows). Replaces the current loan_applications table for read purposes.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent. Can be denormalized for query performance. This is what the UI reads from."},{"name":"Snapshot Store","responsibility":"Periodically stores serialized aggregate state at a known event sequence number to avoid replaying the full event history on every command. Snapshots are an optimization, not a source of truth.","boundaries":"Optional cache layer. Can be rebuilt from events. Critical for adding new audit dimensions retroactively and for disaster recovery of read models.","boundaries":"Batch infrastructure. Must handle large event volumes efficiently. Should be idempotent."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Loan Command Service","contract":"POST /loans/{id}/commands/{commandType} — payload varies by command type (SubmitApplication, UpdateField, ChangeStatus, AssignOfficer). Supporting contexts (user management, notifications) stay simple. Team can adopt incrementally.","sacrifices":"Two persistence paradigms to maintain. Must be disciplined about which contexts use which approach.","alternatives_considered":["Full event sourcing across all contexts — unnecessary complexity for generic/supporting subdomains. No changes to the write side needed. Build upcaster infrastructure before you need it. Establish event schema review as part of the PR process. Never modify existing event schemas — always create new versions."},{"risk":"Eventual consistency confuses users (e.g., officer changes status but dashboard still shows old status)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use read-your-own-writes pattern: after a command succeeds, return the expected new state directly from the command response (or poll the read model with a minimum version expectation). Most projections will update within milliseconds."},{"risk":"Storage growth from retaining all events forever","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Events are typically small (< 1KB each). A loan application with 200 events over its lifetime is ~200KB. Even millions of applications produce manageable storage. Use compression on the event payload column. Archive old events to cold storage if needed, but never delete."},{"risk":"Projection rebuild time grows as event volume increases","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition events by aggregate type. Build projections with parallel consumers. Use snapshots for frequently-accessed aggregates. Projection rebuilds are infrequent (only needed for schema changes or new projections) and can run as batch jobs."},{"risk":"Regulatory requirement to delete personal data (e.g., GDPR right to erasure) conflicts with event immutability","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Use crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in events with per-customer keys. To 'delete' data, destroy the encryption key. The events remain but PII becomes unrecoverable. This must be designed in from the start — retrofitting is extremely difficult. Confirm with legal whether financial services regulations exempt loan data from deletion requirements (many do for the loan lifecycle period)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement event sourcing for the LoanApplication aggregate with a PostgreSQL-backed event store (single events table with aggregate_id, sequence_number, event_type, event_data JSONB, actor_id, timestamp, correlation_id). Add a unique constraint on (aggregate_id, sequence_number) for optimistic concurrency. Restrict table grants to INSERT only. Build two projections: current-state (replaces existing loan_applications table for reads) and audit-trail (optimized for compliance queries). Phase 2: Add point-in-time reconstruction query support to the audit projection. Phase 3: Add event schema versioning and upcaster infrastructure. Run the old CRUD system in parallel during migration, with a reconciliation job comparing states.","files_to_create_or_modify":["events_table.sql — DDL for the event store table with INSERT-only grants and proper indexing","loan_application_aggregate.{ext} — Aggregate root with state machine, event application methods, and command handlers","event_store_repository.{ext} — Repository that loads aggregates by replaying events (with snapshot optimization) and appends new events with optimistic concurrency","domain_events.{ext} — Event type definitions with versioning (ApplicationCreated_v1, FieldUpdated_v1, StatusChanged_v1, etc.)","current_state_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes latest loan application state into a queryable table","audit_trail_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes full change history with field-level diffs and point-in-time reconstruction support","projection_checkpoint.{ext} — Tracks last-processed event position per projection for reliable catch-up","command_handlers.{ext} — HTTP/API layer that deserializes commands, loads aggregates, and invokes command methods","migration_reconciliation_job.{ext} — Temporary: compares event-sourced state with legacy CRUD table to validate correctness during migration"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each command handler follows: load aggregate (from snapshot + events) → validate command → apply domain logic → emit events → append to store → return success. Projections follow: subscribe to event stream → for each event, update read model → checkpoint position. All events are immutable value objects with version annotations. Aggregate protects all invariants — no business logic in command handlers or projections.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in projections — projections are dumb transformers that map events to read model state","Do NOT allow direct writes to read model tables — they must only be updated by projections","Do NOT skip optimistic concurrency — without it, concurrent commands can produce inconsistent event streams","Do NOT store derived data in events — events should capture facts (what happened), not computations","Do NOT use the event store as a message bus — if you need to notify other systems, publish events to a separate message broker after they are persisted","Do NOT forget to include actor identity (who) and correlation ID (why/which request) in every event","Do NOT make event schemas nullable 'just in case' — be explicit about what each event version contains","Do NOT underestimate the migration — run old and new systems in parallel with reconciliation before cutting over"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong fit for immutable audit trail requirements in financial services","CQRS with separate audit and operational read models is the right separation","LoanApplication is the correct aggregate boundary","Crypto-shredding is the standard approach for PII in event-sourced systems","Hybrid approach (event sourcing only where needed) reduces unnecessary complexity"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choices (EventStoreDB vs PostgreSQL-backed vs framework) depend on the team's stack and expertise which is unknown","Whether GDPR right-to-erasure applies to this loan data (jurisdiction and regulation dependent)","Expected event volume and whether snapshotting is needed from day one vs. added later","Team's current skill level with event sourcing patterns"],"assumptions":["The team uses a relational database (PostgreSQL assumed) and can implement the event store as a table rather than requiring a purpose-built event store product","Loan applications have a bounded lifecycle (months, not decades) keeping per-aggregate event counts manageable","The compliance team's query patterns are primarily per-application (not cross-application analytics), which aligns well with aggregate-partitioned event streams","The platform is not already using an event-driven architecture — this would be a new paradigm for the team"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for this loan application audit trail requirement. The compliance mandate for immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, and field-level change tracking aligns precisely with event sourcing's core strengths. However, full event sourcing introduces significant operational complexity that may be overkill when the primary driver is audit compliance rather than complex domain behavior. I recommend a hybrid approach: event sourcing for the loan application lifecycle (where the audit trail is the primary read model) combined with traditional CRUD for supporting contexts like user management. This delivers the compliance guarantees without forcing event sourcing onto the entire platform.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts loan application mutations (status changes, data updates, officer assignments). Validates commands against current state and business rules, then emits domain events. Rejects invalid transitions (e.g., approved → submitted).","boundaries":"Owns the write side of loan applications. No direct database reads for queries. Loads aggregate state by replaying events from the event store."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all loan application domain events. Each event includes aggregate ID, sequence number, event type, payload (changed fields with before/after values), actor ID, timestamp, and correlation ID. Supports optimistic concurrency via sequence numbers.","boundaries":"The single source of truth for loan application state. No UPDATE or DELETE operations permitted. Schema enforcement at the database level (immutable table with no update/delete grants)."},{"name":"Audit Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the event stream into compliance-friendly views: full change history per application, point-in-time state reconstruction, field-level diff reports, actor audit trails. Optimized for the queries the compliance team actually runs.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent with the event store. Rebuilt from scratch if corrupted or if new audit dimensions are needed. Read-only."},{"name":"Current State Read Model (Projection)","responsibility":"Materializes the latest state of each loan application for operational use (dashboards, search, officer workflows). Replaces the current loan_applications table for read purposes.","boundaries":"Eventually consistent. Can be denormalized for query performance. This is what the UI reads from."},{"name":"Snapshot Store","responsibility":"Periodically stores serialized aggregate state at a known event sequence number to avoid replaying the full event history on every command. Snapshots are an optimization, not a source of truth.","boundaries":"Optional cache layer. Can be rebuilt from events. Stale snapshots are safe — replay continues from snapshot sequence number."},{"name":"Projection Rebuilder","responsibility":"Tooling to replay the full event stream and rebuild any read model from scratch. Critical for adding new audit dimensions retroactively and for disaster recovery of read models.","boundaries":"Batch infrastructure. Must handle large event volumes efficiently. Should be idempotent."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Loan Command Service","contract":"POST /loans/{id}/commands/{commandType} — payload varies by command type (SubmitApplication, UpdateField, ChangeStatus, AssignOfficer). Returns 202 Accepted with event IDs.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Loan Command Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"append(aggregateId, expectedVersion, events[]) — optimistic concurrency via expectedVersion. Returns new version number or ConflictException.","coupling_type":"tight (intentional — command service owns the store)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Audit Read Model","contract":"Event subscription (poll or push). Events delivered in order per aggregate. Consumer tracks its own checkpoint position.","coupling_type":"loose (async, consumer-driven)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Current State Read Model","contract":"Same subscription mechanism as audit read model. Independent checkpoint.","coupling_type":"loose (async, consumer-driven)"},{"from":"Compliance UI","to":"Audit Read Model","contract":"GET /audit/loans/{id}/history — full event history. GET /audit/loans/{id}/state?asOf={timestamp} — point-in-time reconstruction. GET /audit/loans/{id}/field-changes?field={name} — field-level history.","coupling_type":"loose"},{"from":"Loan Command Service","to":"Snapshot Store","contract":"loadSnapshot(aggregateId) returns (state, version) or null. saveSnapshot(aggregateId, state, version). Best-effort — failures don't block commands.","coupling_type":"loose (optional optimization)"}],"data_flow":"1) User action hits Command Service → 2) Service loads aggregate (from snapshot + subsequent events) → 3) Command validated against current state and business rules → 4) Domain events emitted and appended to Event Store (single atomic write, optimistic concurrency) → 5) Event subscriptions push events to projection handlers → 6) Audit Read Model and Current State Read Model updated asynchronously → 7) Compliance team queries Audit Read Model for history/reconstruction; Operations team queries Current State Read Model for workflows.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing","rationale":"Core pattern. The compliance requirement IS the event log — events are the primary source of truth, not a derived artifact. This makes the audit trail a first-class architectural concern rather than an afterthought bolted onto CRUD."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Natural companion to event sourcing. Separates the write model (event stream) from read models (materialized projections). Allows audit-optimized and operations-optimized read models to coexist without compromising either."},{"pattern":"Aggregate (DDD)","rationale":"LoanApplication aggregate protects status transition invariants and ensures events are only emitted from valid state transitions. One aggregate per loan application, identified by application ID."},{"pattern":"Optimistic Concurrency","rationale":"Prevents lost updates when multiple officers act on the same application. expectedVersion on append ensures no events are missed between load and write."},{"pattern":"Materialized View / Projection","rationale":"Multiple read models built from the same event stream, each optimized for its consumer (compliance vs. operations vs. analytics)."},{"pattern":"Snapshotting","rationale":"Performance optimization for aggregates with long event histories. Loan applications that go through many revisions would otherwise require replaying hundreds of events per command."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":[{"name":"LoanApplication","root_entity":"LoanApplication (identified by applicationId)","invariants":["Status transitions must follow valid state machine (e.g., Draft→Submitted→UnderReview→Approved/Denied/ReturnedForInfo)","Required fields must be present before submission","Only assigned officer can approve/deny","Denied applications cannot be re-approved without a new submission","All mutations must capture actor identity"],"events":["ApplicationCreated","ApplicationSubmitted","FieldUpdated(fieldName, oldValue, newValue)","StatusChanged(fromStatus, toStatus, reason)","OfficerAssigned(officerId)","DocumentAttached(documentId, documentType)","ApplicationApproved(conditions[])","ApplicationDenied(reason)","ApplicationWithdrawn(reason)"],"value_objects":["ApplicationStatus","ApplicantInfo","LoanTerms","Address","EmploymentInfo","CreditScore"]}]},{"name":"Audit & Compliance","classification":"supporting","aggregates":[{"name":"AuditTrail (read model, not a true aggregate)","root_entity":"Projection built from LoanApplication events","invariants":["Immutability — no modification of recorded history","Completeness — every event must be captured","Traceability — every entry must have actor and timestamp"],"events":[],"value_objects":["AuditEntry","PointInTimeSnapshot","FieldChangeRecord"]}]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":[{"name":"User/Officer","root_entity":"User","invariants":["Authentication and authorization"],"events":[],"value_objects":["Role","Permission"]}]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","downstream":"Audit & Compliance","relationship":"Published Language — Loan Application Lifecycle publishes domain events in a well-defined schema. Audit & Compliance subscribes and projects into compliance-specific views. No back-pressure or influence on the upstream model."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","relationship":"Conformist — Loan Application Lifecycle uses user/officer IDs from IAM as-is to record actor identity in events. No translation needed."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Adopt event sourcing for loan application lifecycle","gains":"Immutable audit trail as a first-class citizen (not bolted on). Full point-in-time reconstruction by replaying events to any timestamp. Field-level change tracking built into the event model. Regulatory compliance becomes an architectural property rather than an application-level concern. Retroactive addition of new audit dimensions by replaying history.","sacrifices":"Significant increase in operational complexity vs. current CRUD model. Team must learn event sourcing patterns and pitfalls. Eventual consistency between write and read models requires careful UX handling. Event schema evolution becomes a long-term maintenance concern. Higher storage requirements (all events retained forever).","alternatives_considered":["CDC (Change Data Capture) on existing table — captures row-level changes via database log (e.g., Debezium on PostgreSQL WAL). Simpler operationally but loses semantic meaning (you see column diffs, not domain events). Point-in-time reconstruction requires replaying CDC log which is lower-level than domain events. Viable if the team lacks event sourcing experience.","Audit trigger table — database triggers write to an audit_log table on every INSERT/UPDATE. Simple to implement, but tightly coupled to schema, loses semantic context (why the change happened), and triggers can be disabled or bypassed. Does not naturally support point-in-time reconstruction.","Temporal tables (SQL:2011) — system-versioned tables that automatically maintain history. PostgreSQL doesn't natively support this (requires extensions like temporal_tables). Good for point-in-time queries but doesn't capture actor or intent. Limited to table-level, not domain-level semantics."]},{"decision":"CQRS with separate read models for audit and operations","gains":"Each consumer gets an optimized view. Audit queries don't impact operational performance. New reporting dimensions can be added by creating new projections without touching the write side.","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — a status change might take milliseconds to appear in read models. More infrastructure to operate (projection handlers, checkpoint tracking). Must handle projection failures and replay.","alternatives_considered":["Single read model serving both audit and operations — simpler but forces compromise on query optimization. Audit queries (full history, point-in-time) have very different access patterns than operational queries (current state, search)."]},{"decision":"Hybrid approach — event sourcing only for loan applications, CRUD for supporting contexts","gains":"Limits complexity to where it delivers value. Supporting contexts (user management, notifications) stay simple. Team can adopt incrementally.","sacrifices":"Two persistence paradigms to maintain. Must be disciplined about which contexts use which approach.","alternatives_considered":["Full event sourcing across all contexts — unnecessary complexity for generic/supporting subdomains. User profile changes don't need immutable audit trails."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Read model projections","evolution_path":"New compliance requirements (e.g., new reporting dimensions, new regulatory queries) can be met by adding new projections that replay the existing event stream. No changes to the write side needed. This is the single biggest advantage for a regulated domain."},{"area":"Event schema","evolution_path":"Events should be versioned from day one (e.g., ApplicationCreated_v1). Upcasters transform old event versions to current schema during replay. This allows the domain model to evolve without rewriting history."},{"area":"Integration with external systems","evolution_path":"The event stream can feed downstream systems (credit bureaus, document management, regulatory reporting) via event subscriptions without modifying the core domain."},{"area":"Analytics and ML","evolution_path":"The complete event history is a rich dataset for loan processing analytics, officer performance metrics, and predictive models. New analytical projections can be added at any time."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event store immutability","reason":"Events must never be modified or deleted. This is both a compliance requirement and an architectural invariant. The database schema should enforce this with restricted grants (INSERT only, no UPDATE/DELETE). This rigidity is intentional and valuable."},{"area":"Event schema backward compatibility","reason":"Once an event type is published, its schema becomes a contract. Breaking changes require new event versions and upcasters. Removing fields from existing event types is forbidden. This discipline is essential but adds friction to schema changes."},{"area":"Aggregate identity","reason":"The applicationId is the partition key for the event stream. Changing how applications are identified (e.g., merging two applications) is extremely difficult in an event-sourced system. Get the aggregate boundaries right upfront."},{"area":"Actor identity in events","reason":"Every event must capture who performed the action. This must be enforced at the command service level before events are created. Retrofitting actor identity onto existing events is impossible (immutability)."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Team inexperience with event sourcing leads to implementation mistakes (e.g., mutable events, missing concurrency control, projections that lose events)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in training. Start with a spike/proof-of-concept on a single aggregate. Use a proven event store library or database pattern rather than building from scratch. Consider Marten (if .NET), Axon (if JVM), or EventStoreDB as purpose-built solutions."},{"risk":"Event schema evolution becomes painful over time as the domain model changes","severity":"high","mitigation":"Version all events from day one. Build upcaster infrastructure before you need it. Establish event schema review as part of the PR process. Never modify existing event schemas — always create new versions."},{"risk":"Eventual consistency confuses users (e.g., officer changes status but dashboard still shows old status)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use read-your-own-writes pattern: after a command succeeds, return the expected new state directly from the command response (or poll the read model with a minimum version expectation). Most projections will update within milliseconds."},{"risk":"Storage growth from retaining all events forever","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Events are typically small (< 1KB each). A loan application with 200 events over its lifetime is ~200KB. Even millions of applications produce manageable storage. Use compression on the event payload column. Archive old events to cold storage if needed, but never delete."},{"risk":"Projection rebuild time grows as event volume increases","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Partition events by aggregate type. Build projections with parallel consumers. Use snapshots for frequently-accessed aggregates. Projection rebuilds are infrequent (only needed for schema changes or new projections) and can run as batch jobs."},{"risk":"Regulatory requirement to delete personal data (e.g., GDPR right to erasure) conflicts with event immutability","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Use crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in events with per-customer keys. To 'delete' data, destroy the encryption key. The events remain but PII becomes unrecoverable. This must be designed in from the start — retrofitting is extremely difficult. Confirm with legal whether financial services regulations exempt loan data from deletion requirements (many do for the loan lifecycle period)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Implement event sourcing for the LoanApplication aggregate with a PostgreSQL-backed event store (single events table with aggregate_id, sequence_number, event_type, event_data JSONB, actor_id, timestamp, correlation_id). Add a unique constraint on (aggregate_id, sequence_number) for optimistic concurrency. Restrict table grants to INSERT only. Build two projections: current-state (replaces existing loan_applications table for reads) and audit-trail (optimized for compliance queries). Phase 2: Add point-in-time reconstruction query support to the audit projection. Phase 3: Add event schema versioning and upcaster infrastructure. Run the old CRUD system in parallel during migration, with a reconciliation job comparing states.","files_to_create_or_modify":["events_table.sql — DDL for the event store table with INSERT-only grants and proper indexing","loan_application_aggregate.{ext} — Aggregate root with state machine, event application methods, and command handlers","event_store_repository.{ext} — Repository that loads aggregates by replaying events (with snapshot optimization) and appends new events with optimistic concurrency","domain_events.{ext} — Event type definitions with versioning (ApplicationCreated_v1, FieldUpdated_v1, StatusChanged_v1, etc.)","current_state_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes latest loan application state into a queryable table","audit_trail_projection.{ext} — Event handler that materializes full change history with field-level diffs and point-in-time reconstruction support","projection_checkpoint.{ext} — Tracks last-processed event position per projection for reliable catch-up","command_handlers.{ext} — HTTP/API layer that deserializes commands, loads aggregates, and invokes command methods","migration_reconciliation_job.{ext} — Temporary: compares event-sourced state with legacy CRUD table to validate correctness during migration"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each command handler follows: load aggregate (from snapshot + events) → validate command → apply domain logic → emit events → append to store → return success. Projections follow: subscribe to event stream → for each event, update read model → checkpoint position. All events are immutable value objects with version annotations. Aggregate protects all invariants — no business logic in command handlers or projections.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT put business logic in projections — projections are dumb transformers that map events to read model state","Do NOT allow direct writes to read model tables — they must only be updated by projections","Do NOT skip optimistic concurrency — without it, concurrent commands can produce inconsistent event streams","Do NOT store derived data in events — events should capture facts (what happened), not computations","Do NOT use the event store as a message bus — if you need to notify other systems, publish events to a separate message broker after they are persisted","Do NOT forget to include actor identity (who) and correlation ID (why/which request) in every event","Do NOT make event schemas nullable 'just in case' — be explicit about what each event version contains","Do NOT underestimate the migration — run old and new systems in parallel with reconciliation before cutting over"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong fit for immutable audit trail requirements in financial services","CQRS with separate audit and operational read models is the right separation","LoanApplication is the correct aggregate boundary","Crypto-shredding is the standard approach for PII in event-sourced systems","Hybrid approach (event sourcing only where needed) reduces unnecessary complexity"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choices (EventStoreDB vs PostgreSQL-backed vs framework) depend on the team's stack and expertise which is unknown","Whether GDPR right-to-erasure applies to this loan data (jurisdiction and regulation dependent)","Expected event volume and whether snapshotting is needed from day one vs. added later","Team's current skill level with event sourcing patterns"],"assumptions":["The team uses a relational database (PostgreSQL assumed) and can implement the event store as a table rather than requiring a purpose-built event store product","Loan applications have a bounded lifecycle (months, not decades) keeping per-aggregate event counts manageable","The compliance team's query patterns are primarily per-application (not cross-application analytics), which aligns well with aggregate-partitioned event streams","The platform is not
